replies.author: @johngaughan9399
replies.text:
@NicCrane Alien 2? Do you mean Aliens or Alien 3?

Aliens used some puppets for the xenomorphs but also guys in alien suits. The queen was animatronic but they might have used puppets or miniatures for some of the walking shots - my memory is a little fuzzy here.

Alien 3 was all puppets and miniatures if I remember correctly, for either version of the movie.

Personally, I think some of the best effects are practical with a light pass of CGI to clean things up. Prometheus may have been a bit of a letdown as far as story goes, but the effects were mostly practical with CGI to remove the wires used to move props around on set and it looked pretty well overall.

replies.author: @FleetChadmiralDane
replies.text: ​@Muzzle_Velocity in the book there's an interesting scene between the main character Grace and Strauss the woman directing the entire project to save mankind. They are waiting for the two astronaut teams to show up to their location, and Strauss vents to Grace that she wanted both of the teams to be entirely heterosexual males due to the history of most astronauts being men and hinting towards other common sense things about men being better suited for difficult situations like this, and how she didn't want to risk any potential romantic tension getting in the way of the mission. Grace acts self-righteous and calls out her "sexism", which is hilarious to read because in a scene prior to this one he accepted the harsh reality that dozens of chimpanzees had to be experimented on to make sure the astronauts would be stably sleeping while they traveled in the ship. We have to remember that at this point Grace is still really naive and hasn't really been put through the ringer like what would happen to him later on in space. He hasn't been shaped into a heroic person until much later on after he's been working with Rocky.

author: @michaelvachon1334
text:
I recently read that good science fiction doesn't need to be 100% accurate to be good, but it does need to make sense. Andy Weir truly knows how to make this work. His presentation of science-based story telling
is very engaging with fully developed characters that capture and hold his audience's attention. Fortunately, his stories also translate very well into films. I found both the book and the movie versions of 'The Martian' to be excellent. I've read the book,  'Project Hail Mary', twice now because the wait for this movie made me so impatient. So looking forward to catching this in IMAX. Thanks for adding to my anticipation!

author: @spinetickspressing
text: I went to high school with Andy Weir. He was my friend.  I remember the first time I saw him, it was science class and he was the only one sitting in front and he hijacked the teacher's lesson asking question after question and challenging the teacher on the science.  I was in the same grade and I sat by a guy I knew from middle school.  We laughed at what was happening but the upper classmen who had to repeat the class were pissed and wanted Andy to stop.  Eventually, another friend who had parents who were nuclear physicists introduced us and the 4 of us sat together, but Andy would take over the class now and then.  I remember being in English class with Andy and we sat near each other and hung out, even back then he had writing journals where he wrote stories.  Something happened my senior year, I dont know what and I saw him less, he didn't have a senior photo or sign my yearbook.  He was a core friend and I am very proud of him.
